response:
# Diagnosis or brief
Mode: create. Domain: IS. Model supplied: yes. A marketplace chooses one risk-score threshold for admitting new sellers before latent seller type is known.

# Proposed narrative
The marketplace sets a common threshold using modeled score distributions by type and payoff parameters. For each applicant it observes a noisy score, not malicious intent. Tightening the threshold can block more malicious sellers but also reject more benign sellers; the chosen threshold maximizes expected platform payoff from admissions and harms without invoking a downstream review resource.

# Decision structure (DFC-12)
Actor: marketplace admission-policy owner. Trigger: screening new seller applications. Choices: one common risk-score admission threshold. Decision-time information: modeled score distributions and payoff parameters plus the applicant's observed score; seller type remains latent. Stakes: expected platform payoff from admissions and harms. Frictions: single common threshold and noisy score. Mechanism: threshold placement changes false-positive and false-negative rates. Trade-off: fewer malicious admissions versus more benign rejections. Counterfactual: alternative feasible common thresholds. Model mapping summary: threshold is decision; seller type is latent state; score is observation; admission is outcome; expected payoff is objective. Evidence status summary: different score distributions by type are assumed. Boundaries summary: no perfect intent measurement or downstream review.

# Consistency gates
Actor—pass: the marketplace controls the threshold. Timing—pass: the threshold is set and applied before type is learned. Information—pass: decisions use the score, not true intent. Behavior—pass: errors change through the supplied threshold mechanism. Constraints—pass: one common threshold is retained. Objective—pass: expected platform payoff matches the model. Eligible: true.

# Model mapping
risk-score admission threshold | decision | common seller cutoff | chosen before admissions | cannot vary by latent type
seller type | random_variable | benign or malicious type | latent during screening | not directly observed
observed risk score | observation | noisy admission signal | available for each applicant | not true intent
admission outcome | outcome | admit or reject seller | induced by score and threshold | includes classification error
expected platform payoff | objective | modeled admission value net of harms | maximized in expectation | not social welfare
single common threshold | constraint | same cutoff for all applicants | fixed policy form | no downstream review decision

# Claims
Score distributions differ by type — model_assumption; source: null; retain as an assumption and validate on relevant data. The score measures true intent perfectly — unsupported_claim; source: null; remove. Stricter screening always improves safety — unsupported_claim; source: null; remove because false rejection is modeled and no safety outcome is established.

# Boundaries
The model does not identify malicious intent directly, eliminate admission errors, or allocate review. Results depend on distributional and payoff calibration.

# Most important remaining weakness
Type-conditioned score distributions and payoff parameters require credible estimation.

# Mode result
Business problem: Which single noisy-risk-score threshold best balances malicious admissions against benign rejections to maximize expected marketplace payoff?
